ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Lemon Coriander Soup; A00FL (Soups and broths) used as the closest defensible broader category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Lemon Coriander Soup is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), which is the typical portion served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Lemon Coriander Soup is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for vegetable clear soups, lemon juice, and coriander leaves, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (cabbage, carrots, mushrooms, coriander). The composite per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation. Cholesterol value (6 mg/100g) is noted from input metadata as sourced from a delivery platform and is retained as a plausible trace value consistent with vegetable-based broth; it has been cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy vegetable soup entries and is within expected range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lemon Coriander Soup. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy with clear vegetable soups and broth-based soups documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), which report GI values of 25–38 for clear vegetable broths and low-starch vegetable soups. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 13.0) / 100 = 3.9, where carbohydrate per 250g serving = 5.2 × 2.5 = 13.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (180 mg/100g; ~450 mg per 250g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor sodium intake from this soup, as a single 250 ml serving contributes approximately 450 mg sodium (~20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by WHO); requesting a low-salt preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low glycemic load (~3.9 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lemon Coriander Soup is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of under 4 per standard serving, producing minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation; it can be consumed freely as a starter or light meal component.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.2 g/100g), zero trans fat, and presence of vitamin C and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This soup is cardioprotective in profile, with negligible saturated fat, no trans fat, and meaningful vitamin C (6 mg/100g) and potassium (90 mg/100g) content; sodium should be moderated in individuals with heart failure or salt-sensitive cardiovascular conditions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients: vitamin C and coriander antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The vitamin C from lemon juice and antioxidant phytochemicals from coriander may support reduction of oxidative stress relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; this soup is suitable as a regular dietary inclusion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon Coriander Soup is a light, clear-broth soup popular in Indo-Chinese cuisine across India. It is prepared by simmering finely chopped vegetables — typically cabbage, carrots, and mushrooms — in a seasoned vegetable stock, then finishing with fresh lemon juice and chopped coriander (cilantro) leaves just before serving. The result is a thin, aromatic soup with a bright citrus tang, herbal freshness, and a mildly savory depth. The texture is watery to lightly thickened (sometimes a small amount of cornstarch is added), and the color is pale golden-green. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (~36 kcal/100g), low in fat (0.8 g/100g), and provides modest protein (1.8 g/100g) and carbohydrates (5.2 g/100g). It is a good source of vitamin A (~80 mcg RAE/100g) and provides vitamin C (6 mg/100g) from lemon juice. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content (~180 mg/100g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The soup is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish at lunch or dinner in Indian restaurants and is also consumed as a light snack. In Hindi it is known as नींबू धनिया सूप (Nimbu Dhaniya Soup). It is widely regarded as an immune-supportive, hydrating, and appetite-stimulating dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemon coriander soup?
Is lemon coriander soup good for diabetes?
What vegetables are in lemon coriander soup?
Is lemon coriander soup good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Lemon Coriander Soup is not listed as a discrete entry in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for vegetable soups and individual ingredients, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for clear vegetable soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite vegetable soup preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific soup preparation is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a plant-based vegetable soup with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA SR Legacy vegetable soup entries. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for a vegan vegetable-based soup with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed by ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data. 5. Cholesterol value of 6 mg/100g was flagged in input metadata as sourced from a delivery platform; this value has been retained as it falls within the plausible range for a vegetable broth that may include trace animal-derived stock in some restaurant preparations, and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy vegetable soup entries. Users preparing a strictly vegan version should expect cholesterol to be 0. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on clear vegetable soups in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Lemon Coriander Soup was found in approved sources. 7. Ontology mapped to broader FoodOn 'soup food product' and FoodEx2 A00FL 'Soups and broths' as no exact codes exist for this specific dish.
